interviewStages: System Design (1 round): Design a photo editing app backend. Not just "make it scale" – they care about user workflows, responsive UI, and handling large files.
Behavioral (1 round): Questions about collaboration with designers, handling feedback, and creative problem-solving.
codingTopics: Simulation and math for image ops
systemDesignTopics: Design the backend for a photo-editing app with large uploads, non-destructive edits, and versioning
Design a real-time collaborative canvas where multiple designers edit one document
Design an asset and font sync service across Creative Cloud desktop and web

coding: Given a large image represented as a 2D matrix, implement an in-place transform (rotate/flip) and discuss memory for very large files.
Implement an LRU cache for decoded image tiles with O(1) get/put; explain eviction under memory pressure.
Given a string of layer/filter operations, parse and apply them in order; handle malformed input gracefully with clean, well-named code.
Given a stack of image layers each with opacity and a blend mode, composite them into a final pixel buffer. Discuss ordering, and how you'd keep it fast on a very large canvas.
Implement undo/redo for an editing session using two stacks; discuss bounded history and memory.
behavioral: Adobe values being Exceptional — delivering results that set a new bar. Tell me about a time your work raised the standard for your team.
Adobe builds tools for creators. Describe a time you changed a technical decision because of the user or designer experience.
Genuine means transparency and honesty. Tell me about a time you gave or received hard feedback and what came of it.
Tell me about a project where you collaborated closely with designers or non-engineers. What did you learn?
Describe a time you had to balance code speed against maintainability.
systemDesign: Design the backend for a photo-editing app: large file uploads, non-destructive edits, versioning, and responsive UI for a creative workflow.
Design a real-time collaborative canvas where multiple designers edit the same document. Discuss conflict resolution.
Design an asset/font sync service across Creative Cloud desktop and web clients.
values: Adobe's values are Genuine, Exceptional, Innovative, and Involved. Which resonates most with you, and why?
What's your favorite Adobe product, and what would you change about its UX?
Even as an engineer, why does design sensibility matter to you?
Tell me about a time you anticipated what users would need next before they asked.

⏱ 2 min read

Adobe
adobe.com ↗

My Adobe Interview Experience

Interviewed at Adobe in 2024 for their Creative Cloud team. If you're expecting typical FAANG-style grinding, you'll be surprised – Adobe cares as much about design sense as coding skills.

What Makes Adobe Different

They build tools for designers. Even as an engineer, they want to know you care about user experience and understand design principles. My interviewer asked "What's your favorite Adobe product and why?" – I said Lightroom and talked about the slider UX. That mattered.

The Process

Recruiter Screen (30 min): Standard stuff. They asked about my portfolio and side projects. If you have anything design-related, mention it.

Technical Phone Screen (1 hour): One coding problem (mine was string manipulation) plus questions about previous projects. They went deep on a web app I'd built – asking about architecture, performance, and user experience choices.

Onsite Loop (4-5 rounds):

- Coding (2 rounds): Medium difficulty. Arrays, strings, trees. Standard stuff but they want clean code with good variable names. My interviewer commented on my naming choices – that doesn't happen at Google.

- System Design (1 round): Design a photo editing app backend. Not just "make it scale" – they care about user workflows, responsive UI, and handling large files.

- Project Deep Dive (1 round): Present a project you've built. They asked about design decisions, tech stack choices, and showed me mockups asking how I'd implement features.

- Behavioral (1 round): Questions about collaboration with designers, handling feedback, and creative problem-solving.

Questions You'll Get

- Image Processing: Filters, compression, file formats

- Performance: Handling large files, real-time rendering

- UI/UX: Responsive design, accessibility, user workflows

- Algorithms: Standard coding problems, nothing exotic

- Design Patterns: MVC, observer pattern, factory pattern

What Worked For Me

- Use Their Products: Download Photoshop or Illustrator (free trial). Play with it. Understand what makes it good (and frustrating). I mentioned keyboard shortcuts I used – interviewer loved it.

- Care About Design: Even if you're backend, show you think about user experience. "This API design makes it easier for frontend devs because…"

- Build Side Projects: They care more about what you've built than LeetCode count. I had a photo gallery app – spent 20 minutes discussing it.

- Know Your Fundamentals: 80-100 LeetCode problems is enough. Focus on clean, readable code.

- Understand Creative Workflows: If you know nothing about design tools, you'll struggle to connect with interviewers.

Common Mistakes

I saw someone on Blind fail Adobe because they dismissed design questions as "not technical." Wrong mindset. Adobe engineers need to understand their users (creators).

Also: Don't just optimize for speed. They care about code maintainability and collaboration with designers. My interviewer asked "How would a designer test this feature?" – I hadn't thought about that.

The Culture

Creative, collaborative, less pressure than FAANG. People actually take time off (unlimited PTO that people use). Open offices with design inspiration everywhere. If you hate that vibe, you'll hate Adobe.

Comp: Solid but not FAANG-level. Base + stock + bonus = 10-15% below Google/Meta. But better work-life balance.
